item.title: Re: It Won't Stop Screaming
item.content:encoded: When are we getting the next update because I love this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: always really enjoyed this, its a lot like a thought thats been stuck my head for years. wish there was more, like i wanna know how the poor guy is doing. someone needs to write a tale or two.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article comes from here, and is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Here is a Magic: the Gathering card with amazingly relevant flavor text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God, the ending is so good. It really conveys this sense of sudden urgency, and properly brings home the point that this is not being done for Lecroy's own benefit, or any obligation the Foundation might feel towards him.

I am left with a ridiculous amount of curiosity about what the heck is going to happen, and I'm sure that's the point. I'm very divided on whether I would actually want answers, though, or if it's more vital to keep the unknown nature of this future event(s) intact. Either way, I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 so hard on all the redaction haters.
I love redactions. Let my imagination do the work for me, I have no need to be spoon fed the narrative.
Taking out addenda 2, sure, I guess. I went to the history to read it and it doesn't add much, but it doesn't hurt either.
Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Scars last, tattoos fade. I thought the scar tests were incredible. How does the universe prevent us from proving a paradox? Human error.

item.title: You hooked me
item.content:encoded:
I'm all in.
I loved it, and I need to know more. This is the hook for what could be a very interesting canon. I would LOVE to get a look at any notes you have, and help write (just giving feedback, not actually changing your vision) the rest. Because I NEED MORE. Why is he invincible? Obviously something went wrong because of a decision the Foundation made. The O5s wouldn't help. BLAMMO, he's sent hurdling through time, and ends up somehow unable to die or be harmed. Or maybe the spiders did it? Whatever it is or was… I gotta know.

And to compliment the very BEST part of this SCP… "Retconned human error" is now my VERY FAVORITE form of the universe preventing a paradox. 'Oh, I lost that page.' 'Oops wrong arm.' Brilliant. Genius.

I do not post on discussions much, and I really only read them to find answers, but I am so much happier knowing you have more planned than if you had just spelled it out at the top of this thread. I'll be keeping an eye open for more from you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1
CONG LAT S

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just wondering, why are they testing if it's the same guy by adding yet more scars, rather than e.g. giving him a unique tattoo (maybe a barcode or large QR code) using ultraviolet ink? All the others might have come about as the results of serial testing (screaming maybe because his consciousness is fused together from several different realities, all of which scarified him in different places…), but a clearly artificial tat using otherwise invisible ink would be unlikely to get lost and almost impossible to fake.

item.title: Well done
item.content:encoded: A very interesting article; An excellent premise written in good form for the SCP foundation, with a story that's cleverly unraveled throughout the text. This is certainly one of my favorite SCP articles on the site. Well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's one of those unanswerables. What does a mind do after millions of years of solitary confinement? How long would it take to recover? We don't know, it's never happened (and, of course, couldn't).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm no neurologist, but solitary confinement doesn't end in boredom but in insanity. What is beyond "stark raving mad"? Apparently it's "screams unceasingly until a death that never comes".

And i absolutely love this, btw. It's an excellent example of "immortality sucks" and what i think of a psychological body horror, but without beating you over the head with either.

+2 (once they allow the feature)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wouldn't a human mind, even one suffocating without dying for thousands of years, eventually get bored with its predicament?

If so, is he just screaming because there's all this light and noise and movement, all of a sudden?

And if so, won't he eventually get bored with this, too, and calm the heck down?

It's a brain. Adapting to new inputs is literally the only thing it does, ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks very much, I think all are fixed now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is awesome.

By the way There are a few "4000's" left in.

item.title: Thank You
item.content:encoded:
Following the completion of the 4000 competition, I would like to say an enormous thank you to everyone who has taken the time to read, vote on, and critique my first SCP effort - and particularly to those who convinced me to remove Addendum 2, which seems to have been universally taken as a positive decision.

It has been a lovely introduction to the SCP community and I look forward to contributing more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much for your feedback scpcrnp, that's very kind of you to say. I'm so glad you enjoyed it. So long as a piece of creative writing has brought a few readers enjoyment, I consider it a success.

I'm juggling a few other SCP ideas at the moment, but eventually I do plan to return to Dr. Lecroy and explore just how he comes to end up where he does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I could vote for Most Underappreciated Entry, this would be it. I’d like to take the opportunity to proclaim that the value and quality of an article is not reducible to its rating. The experiments here are memorably clever and the story stuck with me, so much so that I had to go back and search for this article, not at first knowing it was the one I was remembering, but very impressed when I re-discovered it. This is amazing work for your first entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was pretty darn good, though I'll admit to being somewhat put out by the ending. There's all this buildup, but no real indication of what's being hinted at.

This comment was from a previous version, but I found myself agreeing. A ton of buildup, and no payoff. The concept is cliche, on many levels, to be honest. I like the concept, though, and the stylistic execution almost saves it, but too many cliche elements are kept: Generic Standard Male Everyday Foundation Researcher, generic spooky sentiments avoiding any specific future clarifications, an odd aversion to experimentation that might actually clarify some things, not-quite-believable reaction to being sealed away so long — why screaming? why not simple catatonia? — etc.

Even if all those were changed, this feels like it's missing most of a story, and it lacks payoff.

Out of curiosity, I looked at the old addendum. That wouldn't have been enough payoff for me either, FWIW. It's just there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you very much for the feedback, I'm glad I made the change.

+ Ending Thoughts
- Ending Thinkings

I can definitely see where you're coming from about the new ending point. It was actually originally ordered slightly differently, though I believe the proposals were always at the bottom of that section. It's definitely not as punch as "The importance of restoring SCP-4000’s mind has been upgraded to Priority-1" would be, but I don't think it makes a ton of sense any earlier in that second, and that information is more or less is in the SCProcedures already.

The reason I included it at the end was an attempt to a) clarify the earlier references a bit and b) to make the article cyclical, which I thought was appropriate for a semi-stable time loop SCP. It ends with the information that it effectively starts with.

If I was going to alter the final part to finish on Priority-1, I think I'd probably just have to scrap the final explanatory lines. I'm a bit loath to do so though because I feel they do an OK job emphasising that, hey, this new mysterious information is important. Priority-1 does that a bit, but since I just made up the priority system it doesn't necessarily tell the reader exactly how seriously this is being taken.

At least, I've made up this priority system. I don't believe the Foundation already has one, but I may well have missed it.

My system is basically a concept for how resource allocation works based on how important research is. Priority-0 is "If this isn't done in two hours the universe may implode", Priority-1 is "Get this done as soon as possible, throw all the resources at", Priority-2 is "This is very important but doesn't have a real timescale or a very long one, so have lots of resources and do what you can but no need to rush", Priority-3 is "This is highly important, it would be wonderful to get done, but we're not going to collapse if it doesn't succeed", Priority-4 is "This is something we should be working on, but its unlikely to succeed or the benefits aren't worth excessive cost", Priority-5 is "This is a good thing to work on, but not that important", and Priority-6 is "Meh. Do it. Don't do it. No one's going to be riding you about this one. Just put in some research notes every few days."


Anyway, I think I'll leave the ending the same for the moment but I would really appreciate thoughts on the matter

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looking through the history, removing the second addendum was the right move. On that note, it's left ending off an an underwhelming series of lines about accepted proposals. I believe it would read better with these lines elsewheres - perhaps appended to the SCProcedures.

This would then end on "The importance of restoring SCP-4000’s mind has been upgraded to Priority-1." which feels like a more decent point to emphasize as a final line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you, that's really helpful feedback, and very kind of you. I'm glad the change has had a positive impact (at the very least, I haven't had a down vote since changing it).

I'm also pulling together my first Tale right now, which uses a little of what Addendum 2 might have led to, so it isn't going to waste completely.

And thank you very much for the spelling fixes, they've now been dealt with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With the current iteration of the article being my first exposure to it I believe you made the right choice in removing the addendum. In it's current incarnation I don't feel like I would have missed out if I hadn't gone back and viewed the previous revision. Just my two cents on the matter, maybe someone else has a good idea on how to improve it.

Some minor spelling mistakes I noticed
– hide block

SCP-4000 is believed to have spent approximately four million years burred underground

After detailing its location he was administers with Class B amnesiacs

I'm very interested in seeing how this idea develops and I hope that if I ever start contributing to the site my first post will be of as high a quality as this.

item.title: An Update
item.content:encoded:
Update – 02.08.2018

Thanks to everyone who's provided their thoughts and comments since the original posting.

+ Explanation
- Explanation

So, due to pretty consistent feedback I’ve spent the last couple of hours tinkering with Addendum 2. I’ve come up with a version which I think improves on the original in several areas, but ultimately I’ve reached a slightly more radical conclusion.

I think it might be to the benefit of the SCP to simply remove Addendum 2 altogether. Even my revised version doesn’t really do what people seem to want of it. The last section of an SCP is often the ‘punch’ and while Addendum 2 was not intended to be this, having it as the ending seems to be diffusing the intrigue generated by the main article rather than acting as the ‘I want to learn more’ cliff-hanger I intended it to be.

Moreover, Addendum 2 does not initially appear to be particularly relevant to the majority of this SCP. While it is set up by an earlier section of the document, it doesn’t really elaborate on anything further and instead just creates more questions. Which can absolutely work – creating frustrating unanswered questions is still a major theme of this SCP – but given that Addendum 1 also does this, having it again in Addendum 2, and with far less context, is perhaps not a very effective feature.

In creating Addendum 2, I think I was working by what I considered interesting in the overall narrative I’m trying to create rather than focusing on what is important to this specific piece of writing.

So, I’m removing Addendum 2 for now. I do still have both the original and the updated version if people feel this isn’t a change for the better, or that some part of Addendum 2 is worth salvaging. Opinions on the matter are very welcome.

In order not to completely lose my Site-41 breadcrumb dropping, I’m making a small addition to Addendum 1 in its place. A full log of changes is below.


+ Changes
- Changes
  • Addendum 2 has been removed.
  • An additional reference to Site-41 has been added to SCP-4000-B
  • A comment on this reference, and an already existing one, has been added to Carter’s second report, essentially conveying what would have replaced the final redacted line in the previous version.
  • The [DATA EXPUNGED] used to conceal the identity of the individual prohibiting a line of investigation has been changed to O5-██ to give a slightly bigger hint at what is not being investigated.
  • The line “I hardly need to spell out the importance of the information it could potentially provide to the Foundation” has been removed for being clichéd and a bit trite.
  • Half a dozen sentences have received minor tweaks to make them scan a little better.

As always, feedback is welcome and appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your feedback, it's useful to get some negative criticism too.

I haven't come across 1636 before. Having read it I can't say I'm a particularly big fan personally, but I appreciate it as an interesting point of comparison.

Alterations to Addendum 2 are coming soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much.

item.title: Re: It Won't Stop Screaming
item.content:encoded: I think that final redaction is counter-effective to that feeling of uncertainty you were going for. It shows the the foundation knows something, even if the reader doesn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Going to be blunt, this underwhelmed me.

Not only is the historical connection here fairly tangential at best as I see it, but the base anomaly of someone who can't be damaged has been done before, and in a far more interesting narrative ala SCP-1636.

On top of that, the build-up here is constant but doesn't really pay off. I get that there is a disaster looming for Site-41, (or that a disaster hit Site-41 as the ending seems to imply) but the fact that you wholesale redact the final punch just takes all the wind out of your sails and leaves me shrugging and feeling like my time has been slightly waisted.

-1. Perhaps if you input some of the data behind the redactions the build-up might work out, but I don't know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hope your mother ends up ok op!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks.

If anyone else does have some feedback on this it would be very welcomed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure how I would talk about the burial if I hadn't already explained why it was possible.

Not at all! You start with "he's been buried under rock for millions of years", to which the reader goes, "Wait, what?" and then you explain how. The buildup just isn't as interesting by itself.

See what others think, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback TL333s.

I've given a fuller outline of the ending and my reasons for writing it the way that I did in my reply to Werdna. Can I ask which bit you found particularly problematic? I'm considering changing some elements.

It's a good thought, and honestly I'd have liked to write it that way. Really, the idea came to me from the moment of discovery, so the first two sections are built backwards from that. But the SCP style seems to me to make it difficult to change things around too much.

I'm not sure how I would talk about the burial if I hadn't already explained why it was possible. If the first thing you read was that he'd been stuck underground, it might feel a bit out of place. Plus, having the 'can't lose consciousness' thing after it rather than before it takes away a bit of the horror of the situation. And SCPs generally begin with a physical description rather than situation or powers. As I have it now, the physical description of scars leads into the physical invulnerability, which leads to lack of losing hair, which leads to lack of ageing.

If I could think of a way of writing it in your order without making it feel too non-clinical, I would, but I'm not sure how I'd go about it right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was pretty darn good, though I'll admit to being somewhat put out by the ending. There's all this buildup, but no real indication of what's being hinted at.

I have one big suggestion for you: be more up-front about the "buried for four million years" thing in the description. You front-load the description with all the stuff that can't affect this guy, and it comes off like a bog-standard X-man skip that way. The age and his condition are far more interesting; start with that, then mention he's impervious to injury and doesn't age, then tell us he looks exactly like a current Foundation researcher. I recommend leaving that part for last because it's a good segue into the meat of the story here, and it just suggests so much.

item.title: Re: It Won't Stop Screaming
item.content:encoded:
Ah, thank you so much.

I did a Replace All for SCP-XXXX but I managed to forget the Experiments also used XXXX.

Much appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you enjoyed it Praise Sigmar.

I've given a fuller explanation of the ending in my reply to Werdna.

item.title: Re: It Won't Stop Screaming
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much, that's very kind of you to say! I'm delighted you enjoyed it.

+ Show Block
- Close

1) Re: The title. Yeah, that's totally understandable. 'It Won't Stop Screaming' was the initial name I began with when I first came up with the idea. At that point my intention was to focus much more heavily on SCP-4000 and its psychological damage, with more in-depth physical and psychological tests and with the future mystery parts as the flavour surrounding it. In the end, that didn't quite end up working and the mystery became the main focal point of the text with only two references to SCP-4000 constant screaming remaining. However it was pretty close to the deadline by the time I finished and I couldn't think of anything else to call it, so I left the name.

I'm happy to edit it into something a little less edgy if anyone has any good suggestions.

2) Re: Redaction and Expunging. Thank you for your feedback, that's most helpful.
I do indeed know every piece of information I've concealed in this document (with the exception of dates) including what every part of SCP-4000-B originally said so that all the odd letters would be in the right places. But a problem with that is that since I do know everything I don't get to experience what it's like coming to the document without that knowledge.

Part of the frustration at the end is intentional, although my aim was to make it interesting rather than off putting. One of the major focuses of the document is the uncomfortable feeling of knowing that something is coming, but not quite being able to use that knowledge to do anything. The Foundation is teased with knowledge they can't get access to and which might end up being part of a stable time loop, and I wanted to give the reader a little bit of that feeling too, to give them a kind of cliffhanger.

While I was writing this entry my mother got some not very good medical news, so the frustration of 'an unpleasant future you can't do anything to affect and just have to wait for' was very much on my mind.

I should also probably mention that, while it might be a little self-indulgent for my first SCP, part of SCP-4000-B is also foreshadowing. I have a pretty shrewd idea of how Dr. Lecroy ends up where he does and what happens to him on the way, and I'm intending to reveal that story through a number of other SCPs and quite possibly a Tale or two. If I keep to plan - and of course if my work goes down well enough for people to want to see more - every part of B should be explained at some point.

However, that's definitely not an excuse for anything being unclear or off-putting. Any SCP should be able to stand by itself and making it interesting an appealing by itself is more important than lore building.

I can't really change the [DATA EXPUNGED] in Carter's second report because it would end up referencing something that wasn't very clear to the reader anyway, and if I elaborated further it would probably end up feeling like I'd gone off on a complete tangent since it doesn't have a clear link to SCP-4000's condition.

However, given that two people have found the redaction at the very end confusing I'm considering removing it, or at least part of it.

The reason I've picked on Site-41 is that I found it to be the most enjoyably inconsistent, canon-conflicting Foundation facility of all the sites I looked at. I was searching out if any Sites had a particular theme to them (like 17's connection to psychology, which is why I put SCP-4000 there) when I came upon some references to Site-41.

So far, I have discovered that it is in a frigid arctic landscape and has an SCP containment greenhouse. It's home to a major operations team within the Foundation and is also virtually devoid of people and partly abandoned. It been abandoned and replaced with Site-41-B, and also destroyed in a completely different way. It was constructed SCP-567 and also is SCP-1312. It is located in Japan, and also located in Leningrad during WWII.

I know there isn't just one Foundation canon, and that differences can also be put down to 'misinformation' but Site-41 attracted me as being almost intentionally chaotic. I thought it merited a reason for its many, many contradictions. And that is, currently, what I believe the final redaction covers. If I were going to remove it, I'd either take it out all together or change it to a comment about how data on Site-41 is confusing to ascertain but that official Foundation records state that the current Site-41 is indeed the only one there has ever been.

I'll leave it for the moment, but if anyone else has any feedback on the ending and thinks changing it would be a good idea, please let me know.

Thank you.

Please collapse long posts ~ Leveritas

item.title: Re: It Won't Stop Screaming
item.content:encoded: You still have SCP-XXXX left in some places, please, fix that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea what this was about but it was a good read anyways. Mind explaining the ending?

item.title: Re: It Won't Stop Screaming
item.content:encoded:
This is incredible for a first contribution. I loved it. I'm happy to be one of the first to upvote it.

The only thing which really bugs me is the expungement at the end. I'm not sure what you had in mind (ideally, you should have an idea of what you are expunging, rather than just doing it for looks) but it seems like it might be important information. By taking it out, not only are you implying something has happened to Site-41, but more importantly are making the overt statement that the mere thought Lecroy is correct is vastly important, regardless of the actual nature of the previous iteration of Site-41. I'm not sure that statement alone is convincing. The expungement in 4000-B is a bit more forgivable, since is more subtle.

But still, +1. This kept my attention since I started reading it.

Also, with a title like "It Won't Stop Screaming" I expected some cliche article with a rating of -14 written by a 12-year-old kid who wanted to write a crazy screaming monster. I'm glad this wasn't the case; you really took the time to write a good article.

item.title: It Won't Stop Screaming
item.content:encoded:
This is my first contribution to this site. I do hope you find it enjoyable.

My thanks to Sad Xiao for their feedback on my first draft and to the various people off-site who were kind enough to let me bug them until they ran a critical eye over it. And who will not, of course, get to see this.

And further thanks to a Mr. Paul Rowe whose public domain image I have made use of.
